title: Comfrey leaf poultice for muscle strain
subtitle: An external wound-supportive poultice for minor soft-tissue injury
excerpt: Fresh or rehydrated dried comfrey leaf mashed into a paste and wrapped in muslin. Applied externally to an unbroken, strained muscle or minor soft-tissue injury.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, comfrey entry: the external poultice for bruises, strains, and sprains. the herbal reference on Symphyti radix et herba, traditional use for muscle strains and sprains as an external application.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Prepare immediately before use. The poultice should be applied while the herb is still moist.

glossaryTerms.definition: A mashed or paste preparation of fresh or rehydrated herb applied directly to the skin, typically wrapped in cloth. Active constituents enter the tissue through the skin.

content.text: applies comfrey's allantoin and soothing gel directly to strained or bruised muscle. The herb is for external use only; never take it internally. A poultice works through intact skin without entering the body. The herbal tradition documents its use for sprains, strains, and bruises. Fresh leaf mashed between the palms is the quickest form. Dried leaf rehydrated with a little warm water makes an equivalent paste.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

External use only. Do not take comfrey internally. The pyrrolizidine alkaloids in the leaf are harmful to the liver when ingested.

Not on broken skin. Do not apply to any broken, open, or infected skin. The herb closes surface tissue rapidly; applying over an open wound can trap infection.

Not for prolonged use. Maximum 10 days' continuous use on the same site. No more than 4-6 weeks total use per year.

Muscular injury that needs medical attention. A strain with severe pain, significant swelling, or complete loss of movement following injury may be a serious muscle tear or fracture; go to A&E or your GP before applying any preparation.

items.prepNote: dried comfrey leaf; or a large handful of fresh comfrey leaf if available
warm water to rehydrate dried leaf; not needed if using fresh

content.text: Dried leaf: place the dried comfrey leaf in a small bowl. Pour over just enough warm water to soften it (about 50 ml). Leave for 5 minutes until the leaf is pliable. Drain off any excess water. Mash the rehydrated leaf between clean hands until it forms a rough paste. Fresh leaf: bruise the leaves between clean hands until paste-like.

Place the mashed herb in the centre of a double layer of muslin cloth. Fold the cloth to enclose the herb and form a pad. Apply the poultice pad to the affected area. Hold in place with a light bandage or cloth wrap. The poultice should be comfortably warm, not hot. Leave for 30 minutes, then remove and discard the spent herb. Apply up to three times a day.

Apply for 30 minutes per session, up to three times a day. Use for no more than 10 days continuously on the same site. the European herbal standard guidance: total use no more than 4-6 weeks per year.
When not to use this remedy
Broken skin. Open wounds. Any injury with severe pain, marked swelling, or significant loss of movement needs medical assessment before herbal treatment.
